---
title: "Stretch Task"
subtitle: "Unit 3 Stretch Task"
author: "Ella Patten"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
import sqlite3
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
sqlite_file = 'lahmansbaseballdb.sqlite'
# this file must be in the same location as your .qmd or .py file
con = sqlite3.connect(sqlite_file)
```

## Question 1
__Identify the top 10 players with the longest Major League Baseball careers. Include their:__
__playerID__
__first_name__
__last_name__
__career_length__

Here I find the first and last year of each player's carrer and then find their career length. Then I limit the table to 10 rows and sort by carrer length descending.

```{python}
q = """
SELECT
    playerID,
    MAX(yearID) - MIN(yearID) AS career_length
FROM appearances
GROUP BY playerID
ORDER BY career_length DESC
LIMIT 10
"""
career = pl.read_database(q, con)

q = """
SELECT playerID, nameFirst, nameLast
FROM people
"""
people = pl.read_database(q, con)

top10 = career.join(people, on="playerID").select(
    "playerID", "nameFirst", "nameLast", "career_length"
)
print(top10)
```

## Question 2
__Write an SQL query that pulls the tables you need into a polars dataframe. Then, use python polars to create a summary table showing the average salary for each position (e.g., pitcher, catcher, outfielder). Position information can be found in the fielding table in the POS column.__
__Include the following columns:__

__position__

__average_salary__

__total_players__

__highest_salary__

__The highest_salary column should display the highest salary ever earned by a player in that position.__

__Additionally, add a new column to the summary table you just created to label the average salary:__

__If the average salary is above $3 million, categorize it as “High Salary.”__
__If the average salary is between $2 million and $3 million, categorize it as “Medium Salary.”__
__Otherwise, categorize it as “Low Salary.”__
__Order the table by average salary in descending order.__

Here our query got a little more complicted. First I brought in my salary table to find the salary of each player, based on salary and year. Then I bring in the fielding table where I can find the position of each player. 
If the player played more than one position during the year, I find they poisition they played the most games in and use that as my data point. If it comes in as equal, I sort alphabetically and take the first one. This makes sense to me because the player would most likely be paid based on the position they played the most.
Then we group them together to find the averages and total players. Finally, I categorize the salaries based on the criteria and print the table sorted descending by average salary.

```{python }
# salary table
salary = """
SELECT playerID, yearID, salary
FROM salaries
"""
salaries = pl.read_database(salary, con)

# fielding table
fielding = """
SELECT playerID, yearID, POS, SUM(G) AS games
FROM fielding
GROUP BY playerID, yearID, POS
"""
fielding = pl.read_database(fielding, con)

# pick one position per player-year based on most games
primary_position = (
    fielding
    .sort(["playerID", "yearID", "games", "POS"], descending=[False, False, True, False])
    .with_columns(
        pl.arange(0, pl.len()).over(["playerID", "yearID"]).alias("pos_rank")
    )
    .filter(pl.col("pos_rank") == 0)
    .select(["playerID", "yearID", "POS"])
)

# join tables
salary_position = salaries.join(
    primary_position,
    on=["playerID", "yearID"],
    how="inner"
)

summary = (
    salary_position
    .group_by("POS")
    .agg([ # find salaries and total players for each position
        pl.mean("salary").alias("average_salary"),
        pl.n_unique("playerID").alias("total_players"),
        pl.max("salary").alias("highest_salary")
    ])
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("average_salary").round(0).cast(pl.Int64)
    )
    .with_columns( # categorize salaries
        pl.when(pl.col("average_salary") > 3000000).then(pl.lit("High Salary"))
          .when((pl.col("average_salary") >= 2000000) & (pl.col("average_salary") <= 3000000)).then(pl.lit("Medium Salary"))
          .otherwise(pl.lit("Low Salary"))
          .alias("salary_category")
    )
    .sort("average_salary", descending=True)
)

print(summary)
```
